title: LibERC6551 deterministic account address computation via backwards initcode layout and CREATE2 hash
explanation: Two complementary functions: (1) account() predicts the CREATE2 address. The init code is built backwards starting from offset 0xa3 (tokenId) up to 0x00 (proxy prefix), allowing each mstore to extend the buffer without computing exact non-overlapping offsets. shr(96, shl(96, tokenContract_)) cleans the upper 96 bits of the address parameter in case it has dirty bits from ABI decoding. The CREATE2 derivation at the end uses scratch space: 0xff prefix at byte 0, REGISTRY at offset 0x01 (shl(96) places it in bytes [1..21]), salt at offset 0x15, init code hash at offset 0x35. keccak256(0x00, 0x55) hashes exactly 85 bytes = 1 + 20 + 32 + 32. (2) isERC6551Account verifies an address by extcodecopy-ing the appended args (96 bytes from offset 0x0a), extracting the embedded implementation, and reconstructing the init code in memory then re-deriving the CREATE2 address. The check `iszero(shl(96, xor(a, derived)))` compares only the lower 160 bits — equivalent to address equality but slightly cheaper than a full uint256 eq. The pre-check `extcodesize(a) == 0xad` (proxy runtime is exactly 173 bytes) is a fast-fail before doing the expensive keccak.
when_to_apply: Verifying or predicting addresses of accounts deployed via the canonical ERC6551Registry, building inventory tooling, or implementing on-chain ownership cycle detection
when_not_to_apply: Custom ERC6551 implementations using non-canonical registries or different proxy bytecode, in which case the hash check would always fail
